raw_transcript.text: If you want to get better at
cinematography, you've got to practice
all the time. But most of us don't have
easy access to a big crew or actors or a
lot of fancy gear. So, what do we do?
Well, why not practice filming yourself?
In this video, we're going to talk about
how to find inspiration and how to find
interesting locations, which gear to
use, whether you're a beginner or you're
more advanced. Then, I'll take you
through some specific techniques like
shooting with all manual cine lenses. In
this case, I'll be using the DZO film
Vespid 2 kit. I'll show you some special
techniques like filming wide low angle
shots, defocusing yourself in the
foreground, and shooting B-roll with
longer lenses. Then, we're going to dive
into some minimalist lighting techniques
using the tiny Gutox ML100R lights to
add just the right touch to the natural
light in the scene. We'll do a few quick
indoor shots. Then, we'll simulate
daylight outdoors, and then we'll use
the lights to create a neon glow at
night. [music] And finally, I'll show a
few experimental techniques for using AI
to replace the lights with practical
sources for some interesting results.
All right, so let's jump in and learn
how to practice cinematography all by
yourself. This video is sponsored in
part by DZOILM.
If you want to practice cinematography,
the first step is to find the right
inspiration. And the first step to that
is to start thinking realistically about
what you're going to be able to shoot.
You're not going to be able to make Dune
all on your own. [music] We all love
Dune. We would all love to shoot Dune,
but unless your dad owns a studio, you
probably won't start out by shooting
Dune. So, let's find some more
appropriate inspiration. My
recommendation is to find movies shot in
a similar environment to yours. The tool
that I like to use for this is Shot
Deck. So, this is not sponsored. I have
no relationship with Shot Deck at all.
It's just a very useful website for
searching for a specific kind of
location or a specific type of shot and
then finding still frames for movies
that you can use to kickstart your
imagination. Movies have been shot
everywhere and even the most boring
environments have been made cinematic.
So you can search for shots done in
[music] a similar environment to places
that you might be able to access. So for
instance, I found this neon sign and I
took a photo on my phone, saved the
location data so that I'd be able to go
back there and shoot one [music] day. I
searched on Shot Deck for shots done in
Hong Kong. And I found this still frame.
Now, the goal here is not to copy. It's
just to make something that looks good,
even if it's completely different from
the source material. Nobody's ever going
to know where you got your inspiration.
All you have to do is make a shot that
you find interesting. But you may be
thinking, "Okay, but I don't live in
Hong Kong. I live in Topeka, Kansas.
What do I do?" I would start just by
searching Instagram for my city and
scrolling. You might find some
unexpectedly cinematic locations. I like
to find places that seem to be public so
I won't get kicked out for shooting
there and that have some sort of
character or history. Then I look them
up on maps and I check out how they look
in real life. Another search I like to
do is the word hidden and then the name
of my city. It's a great starting point
for finding inspiring locations that not
everybody's going to be using. There's
interesting spots to shoot almost
everywhere on Earth if you just do a
little research.
Okay, now we'll go into the gear that
you can use to practice film making.
Now, I'm going to start off by saying
you could use just about anything. There
aren't really any rules. But my advice
here would be to generally keep it
simple. Make sure you can fit everything
in a backpack. And even if you already
own a lot of gear, only take out a few
pieces at a time. Otherwise, the options
will become overwhelming. So, at least
you need a camera. I've been using the
Sony A7S3 for almost 5 years, but you
can use whatever camera you already own.
And at least you need one lens. And I
recommend a prime lens because they
generally have more character in the
image and they have wider apertures.
I'll be using the DZO Film Invested 2
lenses in this video, but most primes
will work just fine for your practice.
It's more important to pay attention to
[music] what you put in front of the
lens. If you're only going to use one
lens, I recommend starting with a 35mm
f1.8. If you're going to be shooting in
the daytime, you do need a variable ND
filter so you can keep your shutter
locked at 1/50th of a second if you're
shooting 24p or basically double
whatever your shutter speed would be.
It's called 180° shutter. Then, if
you're filming yourself, you will need
some kind of a tripod. I have several
travel tripods and mini tripods I like
to use. I usually don't use a full-size
tripod for my practice shots because
they're just too bulky. And then, if you
want to get more advanced, [music] you
might want to bring along a light. For
this video, I'll be using the ultra
portable and powerful Gutox ML100R,
which is a 100 watt fullcolor RGB video
light with some really interesting
accessories that you can power via USB.
And for your light, you'll of course
need some kind of light stand. I use the
same mini tripods for my light as I do
for my camera. I just sometimes attach
my Shine carbon fiber extension rod on
top of the mini tripod to get some extra
height. So, that's basically all the
gear you need to get started. a camera,
a prime lens, some kind of tripod, and
then maybe a light and some kind of
light stand. All right, let's start
shooting.
[music]
Here's the first shot that I'm going to
break down for you. This is a low
wide-angle shot of myself watching an
airplane fly by. [music]
I shot this on the Vespit 2 18 mm lens
because for this shot, I wanted to show
the size of that massive building behind
me. So, to make it look huge, I needed a
wide-angle lens. Now, we'll go step by
step into how I set up and filmed
[music] this shot. First, I put the
camera on my mini tripod at a low angle.
Then, I tilted up the lens about 45°.
Then, I walked to my position in the
shot and I extended another mini tripod
and I set it down there. I used that
mini tripod as a standin to set my focus
because these lenses are all manual, so
I couldn't just rely on autofocus to
keep me sharp. Then I lowered that mini
tripod, but I left it in place to use as
a mark so I knew where to stand in the
frame. So then I could just walk up to
the tripod and be in perfect focus and
framing in my shot. But there was still
something missing in this composition.
The sky was pretty empty, so I added an
airplane. I just grabbed some random
image of a plane from online and then I
removed the background with one of those
free background removing websites. Then
I dropped that plane image onto my
timeline as a separate layer and I added
some gauian blur to defocus it a bit to
match the background. And I adjusted its
position to make it fly across the
frame. Now the shot really takes
advantage of the depth of the location.
There's action happening in the
foreground all the way to the deep
distance. [music]
Another reason to shoot low and wide is
if you have an interesting sign or
something above you in the background
and you want to feature it and show how
big it is. I set up this shot the same
way as the last one, using my selfie
stick tripod as a stand-in to set my
focus and my framing.
Then I pushed down the selfie stick and
used it as a mark so I knew where to
stand in the frame. This time I put
myself in the lower half of the frame so
that I wouldn't cover up the sign behind
me. Putting myself in the frame below
the sign gave a sense [music] of scale.
Because there's a person in the frame,
you can feel how large the sign is. So,
whenever you're in a location that has
something interesting overhead, you can
try doing this type of low and wide shot
[music] to incorporate yourself into a
composition with a lot of depth.
When filming yourself, it of course
feels natural to always put yourself in
perfect focus, but since we're shooting
with manual lenses, why not get creative
with it? One technique I like to do is
focusing on the background instead of
the subject in the foreground. Not only
does this create an interesting
composition, it also means you don't
have to worry about your subject being
out of focus because they're supposed to
be out of focus anyway. This tends to
work better with telephoto lenses rather
than wide lenses because with the
telephoto lens, you can defocus your
subject [music] more. For this shot, I
used the silhouette of this hallway as a
framing device. Then I positioned myself
in the empty part of the frame where
there are no islands over the water and
[music] I walked straight toward the
camera staying in the same area of the
composition. For this shot, I set up my
tripod facing the water and waited for a
boat. Then once I saw a boat, I ran to
my mark and then I continued walking
across the frame as the boat moved
toward me. This opposing motion makes
the shot more interesting because you
have a complimentary movement on either
side of the frame. Here's another one I
did just a few minutes later. This time,
all the boats crossing the frame created
that opposing motion, and I put myself
in the middle as sort of an audience
surrogate. In other words, the audience
can imagine themselves as me observing
all these boats and being in this scene.
So, the next time you find an
interesting background for your shot,
consider putting yourself out of focus
in the foreground.
[music]
Next, we're going to talk about filming
B-roll moments using a telephoto lens. A
longer lens lets you pick out smaller
moments from the big picture, so you can
find unique compositions that feel more
subjective and more personal. And it
also helps you get more shots in a
shorter period of time because instead
of capturing just a few big wide shots,
you can get a lot of detail shots
instead. When I'm composing these B-roll
shots, I like to look for contrasting
elements like the hard angles of this
building versus the soft texture of the
clouds or the warm colors of the
building versus the cooler tones of the
other buildings in the distance. Here's
another one. Warm colored building, cool
colored sky. This shot combines layers
of buildings to create an abstract
composition. Here, I manually racked
focus from the dolphins in the
foreground to the skyline in the
background. This added some motion to an
otherwise static frame. As sunset became
dusk, I cooled down my white balance to
4100K to let the image go deep blue. And
I just caught random moments as people
enjoyed the view. It certainly helps
having a beautiful city like Hong Kong
as your environment, but you can find
great B-roll shots in any location if
you just look carefully and you find the
right light.
This part of the video is proudly
sponsored by DZOILM. I asked DZofilm if
I could use their Vespit 2 prime lenses
because this is a set of relatively
compact cine primes that deliver a
really sharp and vibrant look which
makes them great as an all-purpose kit
of lenses for filmmakers shooting solo
or shooting with small crews and
especially for people shooting on
location. These are T1.9 lenses which is
the same as f1.8 and they weigh about
800 g each and they have a consistent 77
mm filter thread so you [music] can use
the same filters across the whole set.
They have a very large image circle
covering full-frame Vista Vision sensors
[music] and even some medium format
cameras. Optically, these lenses are
designed to minimize chromatic
aberration, lens breathing, and flare.
So, you get a tac sharp image from edge
to edge at all apertures. So, your shot
looks clean but not clinical. They still
do have a bit of character in the bokeh.
The focus throw is about 270 to 300°
depending on the lens, so they will feel
familiar if you've ever pulled focus
with cine lenses before. One high-end
feature is that they support cook eye
technology, which captures lens metadata
like focus and aperture automatically.
Now, this isn't a feature that I
personally need, but it is a great
timesaver for people who are doing VFX
heavy work. So, these Vespatos are
basically your all-around daily shooting
lenses that you can use for all kinds of
film making when you just need a clean
starting point for your image.
Next, we're going to talk about
practicing lighting by yourself. It can
be tricky to replicate natural light as
a solo shooter. Because to imitate the
sun, you usually need really large light
sources. Hollywood films will literally
use tons of lighting gear for this
purpose. [music] But since we're solo
shooting here, we're going to do things
a much simpler way. Here, I'll be using
the Godox ML100R lights, which are tiny,
portable 100 W RGB lights that have some
amazingly creative accessories.
>> [music]
>> For this first shot, all I needed was a
pop of light to pull me out of the
background of the living room because
the spot where I wanted to stand [music]
was in the shadows. On the ML100R, I put
my reflector first. Then on that, I put
this collapsible rubber parallel beam
adapter, which basically turns it into a
spotlight with a 5° spread. I powered it
by plugging it into the wall via USBC on
my 100 W wall charger. Then I set the
white balance of the light to 4100K to
match the color of the light coming from
the windows. I angled the light down
about 45° and I raised it up on my light
stand. This is an Ulanzi AT1 tripod and
I put a Shinewe carbon fiber rod on top
of it to get extra height. So, with that
ML100R positioned just out of frame, I
was able to get a spot of hard light
right where I needed it. So, here was my
shot with the light off. And now, here's
the shot with the light turned on. Since
the beam of light was so narrow, I
didn't need to use any additional flags
or accessories to control spill. and I
was able to walk from the artificial
light back into the natural light
seamlessly.
Next, I wanted to do a shot on the
beautiful balcony of this house
overlooking the living room because I
had that afternoon light filling up the
whole background. I just didn't have a
spot of light exactly where I wanted to
stand in the frame. So, once again, I
raised up the light with the parallel
beam adapter on it and I focused it on
just the spot where I would be standing
in the shot. This gave me a strong
sidelight that looks pretty natural
because the light is several meters
away, imitating the look of daylight
coming from a window. Then I filmed
myself walking up to the spot and
looking over the edge. And here's the
result. I was able to film this really
wide shot with just one small light
because I didn't worry about lighting
the background. I just focused the light
on me as the subject.
This next shot is inspired by the
naturallook lighting in this still frame
from the film Saltburn. This is the kind
of medium shot that you'll do all the
time for dialogue scenes or interviews.
So, it's a good one to practice, but the
natural light once again was only
falling on the background. I raised up
the light a little bit above my head and
I positioned it off to the side and
slightly behind the chair about 2 and
1/2 m away. Then, with the camera facing
the chair directly, I got this
composition. The light added that extra
dramatic edge on the side of my face to
focus the viewer's attention on me
instead of the background.
Good boy.
>> Now, we're going to take this little
light outside and do the same trick.
This time, I'm going to be using an 85mm
lens to make those distant buildings
feel a bit closer in the frame. I put my
hat on the selfie stick tripod to create
a lighting standin, and I brought out my
light with the parallel beam adapter on
it, and I powered it using this 100 W
power bank.
I put the light a few meters away and I
aimed it at the side of my hat while
using my phone as a wireless monitor
with the monitor and control app.
Then I pushed down the selfie stick and
I stood in its place. My Small Rig
iPhone 17 case has a kickstand, so I set
the phone on the ground angled up so
that I could keep an eye on the
composition the whole time. And here's
the final shot with my fake sunset light
in the foreground and the real sunlight
in the background.
Next, I wanted to put some direct
sunlight on this convenience store to
make it look like a hot summer afternoon
instead of early dusk. I once again set
up my hat as a stand-in, and I turned on
the light. Then, I changed my camera's
white balance to 6900K, making things
look much warmer. The shop owner helped
me out by being a live stand-in, and
then I thanked him, and I took his
place. I walked into frame and grabbed
an ice cold soda from the refrigerator
with the ML100R projecting sunlight from
about 4 m away. And because the parallel
beam adapter has only that 5° spread, it
didn't spill anywhere except where I
needed it, so I didn't need to flag it
off. And the spill light also didn't
bother any other people in the area. And
here's the final shot. All the direct
sunlight in this shot is from that
single 100 W light. [music]
>> This next lighting setup is at night. I
wanted to do a shot featuring this old
flickering neon sign in the background.
The foreground already had enough
ambient light to get a good exposure,
but it didn't feel like the light was
coming from a neon sign. So, I set up my
camera with a 35mm lens on a mini tripod
to get a low perspective. Then, I
brought out my God Doc's inflatable
airsoft tube, which is so much easier to
use and more compact than a softbox. You
just use the strap to tighten it on the
reflector and it's installed. Once
again, I powered it with my power bank,
which I also used to set my mark for
where to stand.
I adjusted the color of the light to be
similar to the neon sign behind me. And
I kept it at a low angle because I
wanted a bit of that unnatural looking
upward glow. And then I set up a second
light with the parallel beam adapter as
my backlight. Since this other light was
far away, I controlled it with Bluetooth
using the Godox light app, which let me
set all the parameters on my phone. And
then I switched over to the monitor and
control app to check the results. So
here you're seeing the final setup.
And here are the results. The lights
added just a bit more color and
character to the shot for that old
school Hong Kong feeling.
All right, we could be done here, but
we're not. There's one more little
lighting trick I've been playing around
with. We're placing the light in post
with something else using AI. So, for
this shot, I set up the light with the
inflatable airsoft tube on the table
behind me. I wanted the light to be in
frame to simulate a practical source.
So, I exported a still frame and I
brought it into Google Gemini and I
prompted to replace it with a desk lamp
and some scattered newspapers like a
film noir movie. I brought that still
image back into Resolve and I added it
as a layer on top of my original
footage. Then I did a rough mask in the
color tab, added an alpha output, and
basically created a split screen where
the AI generated image just covers the
light and the table. So, here's the
final result. The Godox light became a
real desk lamp.
Okay, one more example. This one's a
little wilder. I put the same light in
the middle of my bed, and I set it to
the fire effect preset.
Then I exported a still image, imported
it into Cling, and I prompted it to
generate a video replacing the light
with a fire. It took a lot of tries once
again to get something usable, and I
burned a bunch of credits. Eventually, I
got something good enough. So, I brought
it into Resolve and dropped it on top of
my original footage as another layer.
Then, I made a quick mask in the color
tab so the AI generated video only
covered the left part of the frame.
Then, I used the cinematic haze effect
on top to create some ambient smoke in
the room. On top of that, I added an
adjustment layer with some camera shake.
And here's the final result. It may look
chaotic, but this was 100% safe to
shoot.
Personally, I prefer doing AI this way
with the split screen technique rather
than doing fully AI generated video
because the most important part of the
shot, the person is [music] still real.
Okay, those are some tips and techniques
for practicing cinematography all by
yourself. I want to say thank you to
DZOIL for sponsoring part of the video.
I also want to thank Godox for letting
me use their ML100R lights. If you want
to know more about the gear or learn
more from me, there will be links in the
description. So, thanks so much for
watching and I will see you next
